data.nf11: Professor Yang has made seminal contributions at the forefront of materials science and energy engineering, particularly in the development of next-generation battery technologies beyond lithium-based systems. Her research on sodium-ion and zinc-ion batteries has established new scientific paradigms for scalable, cost-effective, and sustainable energy storage—technologies critical to the post-lithium era. Her work integrates nanomaterials design, electrochemical mechanisms, and device-level engineering to position Singapore as a global hub for sustainable energy materials research.

•	High-Impact Publication Record: She has authored over 450 high-impact peer-reviewed publications in leading international journals such as Nature Communications, Advanced Materials, and ACS Nano.
•	Global Influence: Her work has received more than 32,000 citations with a h-index more than 100, demonstrating a profound and sustained influence on the global scientific and engineering communities.
•	World-Class Ranking: She has been consistently ranked among the top 2% of scientists worldwide on Stanford University’s authoritative list in every update since 2019.
•	Prestigious Accolades: Her excellence has been recognized through the NRF Investigatorship (2025), the SG 100 Women in Tech List (2025), the ACS Nano Impact Award (2024), and the SNIC–AsCA2019 Distinguished Women Chemist Award (2023).
•	Research Funding: She has secured and managed over SGD 28 million in competitive research funding from national agencies and industry partners. As Principal Investigator, she has successfully completed more than 40 research projects, bridging fundamental nanomaterials research with applied, industry-relevant outcomes.
•	International Collaboration: She has led large-scale international research programs that strengthen Singapore’s global research networks and promote shared infrastructure.
data.nf14:
Professor Yang has played a sustained role in shaping Singapore’s STEM landscape for over 16 years as an independent principal investigator:
•	Institutional Capacity Building: During her tenure at the Singapore University of Technology and Design (SUTD) (2010–2025), she progressed from Assistant Professor to Full Professor and founded the Energy and Environmental Sustainability Laboratory.
•	Strategic Alignment: Since joining NUS in August 2025, she has continued to strengthen national capabilities in nanomaterials for energy and environmental applications, aligned with Singapore’s strategic priorities.
•	Scientific Ambassadorship: As an elected Fellow of the Royal Society of Chemistry (RSC), Academician of Asia Pacific Academy of Materials (APAM), Fellow of International Association of Advanced Materials (IAAM), Fellow of Institute of Materials, Minerals & Mining (IMMM) and the ASEAN Academy of Engineering and Technology (AAET), she serves as an international ambassador for Singaporean science.
data.nf16:
•	Outreach and STEM Promotion: Professor Yang’s contributions extend significantly to the development of future scientific leaders, a core priority of the Singapore National Academy of Science. She actively participates in numerous Ministry of Education (MOE)–led national and international outreach programs, such as the MOE Science Mentorship Programme, where she has personally mentored over 30 secondary school, junior college, and polytechnic students to inspire early interest in STEM disciplines.
•	Advocacy for Diversity and Inclusion: Professor Yang is a prominent advocate for diversity and inclusion in science, actively encouraging female students to pursue careers in STEM. For over a decade, she has served as an ambassador for the Singapore UN Women’s STEM program. Her commitment to this cause is further exemplified by her own recognition as a L’Oréal Singapore For Women in Science National Fellow.
•	Exceptional Commitment to Mentorship: Professor Yang has demonstrated a profound commitment to high-level academic mentorship, having supervised more than 100 PhD students and postdoctoral researchers. These individuals now contribute meaningfully across academia, national research institutes, and industry.
•	Global Academic Impact: Reflecting her role in shaping the global scientific community, more than 30 of her former mentees have established independent research groups as faculty members at leading universities worldwide. This "academic progeny" significantly amplifies Singapore’s international research influence and ensures a lasting legacy in the field of materials science.

data.nf10: Self Healing Inverter, Fault diagnostics, Power quality in micro grid
data.nf11:
The researcher has demonstrated significant leadership and excellence in securing and executing funded research projects in the domain of power electronics, renewable energy, and smart grid technologies. As Principal Investigator, they have successfully led multiple projects sponsored by prestigious agencies such as the Department of Science and Technology (DST), University Grants Commission (UGC), and IIT Palakkad Technology IHub Foundation. These projects focus on advanced areas including multilevel inverters, solar photovoltaic systems, universal modular inverters, and microgrid architectures aimed at improving power quality, efficiency, and fault tolerance in renewable energy applications.
Notably, the researcher contributed to the development of smart grid architectures with self-healing capabilities and solar PV-assisted microgrid systems for rural electrification. Their involvement extends beyond individual projects, serving as Co-Principal Investigator in high-impact initiatives such as the establishment of a Renewable Energy Research Center under the DST-FIST scheme and interdisciplinary research funded by the Indian Council of Medical Research (ICMR) and ANRF, with funding reaching up to Rs. 2 crores.
International exposure and recognition further strengthen their profile, including the prestigious Raman Research Fellowship to pursue post-doctoral research in the USA and the VIT International (VIN) award for collaborative research in Botswana. Additionally, they have contributed to global research collaborations, including projects with the University of Technology Sarawak, focusing on smart hybrid inverters compatible with international grid standards.
The researcher has secured cumulative funding across diverse projects, demonstrating strong capability in innovation, interdisciplinary collaboration, and technology development. Their work significantly contributes to sustainable energy solutions, smart infrastructure, and the advancement of clean and reliable power systems, positioning them as a leading contributor in the field of modern energy research.

data.nf16:
Dr. S. Albert Alexander stands as a distinguished academician, researcher, and innovator whose contributions to electrical engineering and sustainable energy systems make him a highly deserving candidate for the prestigious International Award. With nearly two decades of academic and research experience, he has consistently demonstrated excellence in advancing renewable energy technologies, smart grids, and intelligent control systems.
His research portfolio is both deep and expansive, reflected in over 180 high-impact publications indexed in leading databases such as Scopus and SCI, with an impressive citation record and h-index. These works have significantly contributed to the global knowledge base in areas such as solar photovoltaic systems, multilevel inverters, electric vehicle charging infrastructure, and AI-driven energy solutions. His ability to blend theoretical rigor with practical implementation has led to the successful development of multiple innovative solar inverter technologies, enhancing power quality and system reliability.
Dr. Alexander’s leadership in research is evident through his role as Principal Investigator and Co-Principal Investigator in numerous funded projects supported by prestigious agencies, including the Department of Science and Technology (DST), UGC, and international collaborations. His projects address critical global challenges such as sustainable energy access, rural electrification, and smart grid resilience, aligning closely with international sustainability goals.
In addition to his research excellence, he has secured patents, authored influential books, and contributed to interdisciplinary innovations spanning energy, healthcare, and artificial intelligence. His international exposure, including a postdoctoral fellowship in the USA and global research collaborations, further underscores his global impact.
Overall, Dr. Albert Alexander’s remarkable achievements, visionary research, and commitment to sustainable technological advancement position him as an exemplary nominee whose work resonates far beyond academia, making him a truly deserving recipient of this international recognition.

data.nf11:
Dr. Jenila Vincent is a passionate GIS enthusiast, educator, and researcher dedicated to promoting open-source geospatial technologies. Her research focuses on remote sensing for agroforestry mapping and urban footprint analysis, integrating advanced geospatial techniques and machine learning models to enhance land use and land cover (LULC) classification accuracy and support sustainable environmental planning.
I am pleased to inform you that Jenila has been awarded as a Winner in the IIT Bombay FOSSEE Geospatial Mapathon 2025 (Edition 05), a joint initiative of FOSSEE GIS, The RuDRA Lab, IIT Bombay, and partner institutions. She mentored Team Participant 1370, titled “Mapping the coastal delineation in Sunderban Forest”  in the FOSSEE Mapathon 2023, jointly organized by IIT Bombay, IIT Tirupati Navavishkar I-Hub Foundation, and TNSDC, where her team was recognized as Notable Participants. She was a speaker at the “From Satellites to Sprouts: Boosting Water Security with Earth Observation” session at the Geo for Good Summit 2025, held from 8–11 September 2025 at the Google Singapore Office, Mapletree Business City II, Singapore. She also presented a research paper titled “Mapping of Fallow Lands in Tamil Nadu Using Multi-Temporal Remote Sensing Analysis” at the Tamil Nadu Land Use 2025 – International Conference on Research & Innovation for a Sustainable Future, held on 6–7 October 2025 at the IIT Madras Research Park, jointly organized by the State Planning Commission, Government of Tamil Nadu, and the Tamil Nadu State Land Use Research Board and this research work is been funded by TNSLURB (19.08 lakhs). She has also collaborated with Raj Bhagat Palanichamy, Senior Program Manager (Sustainable Cities & Transport) at the World Resources Institute India, on research projects involving automation of spatio-temporal analyses in remote sensing.
data.nf12:
Dr. Jenila Vincent has received several recognitions for her academic excellence, research contributions, and professional achievements. She secured University Rank in M.E. from Anna University (2012) and has consistently demonstrated academic distinction throughout her career. She qualified the Tamil Nadu State Eligibility Test (SET) for Lectureship in Computer Science (2016), recognizing her eligibility for higher academic teaching and research positions.

She was honored with the Best Outgoing Student Award during both her undergraduate studies at St. Xavier’s Catholic College of Engineering and her postgraduate studies at Jerusalem College of Engineering, reflecting her outstanding academic performance and leadership qualities. She also received multiple Certificates of Merit and Excellence from Anna University for securing the highest marks in semester examinations.

At the national and international levels, she has been recognized for her contributions to geospatial research. She was a winner in the IIT Bombay FOSSEE Geospatial Mapathon 2025, organized by the FOSSEE GIS initiative at IIT Bombay, for her innovative work on agroforestry mapping using satellite imagery and deep learning techniques. Additionally, her research work was selected for presentation at the Geo for Good Summit 2025 held at Google Singapore, where she presented her work on satellite-based agroforestry mapping using Earth observation technologies.

data.nf14:
Dr. Jenila Vincent is an active researcher in the interdisciplinary fields of remote sensing, geospatial analytics, machine learning, and environmental monitoring. Her research primarily focuses on applying advanced computational techniques to analyze satellite imagery for applications such as agroforestry mapping, land-use change detection, urban mapping, and environmental sustainability.

She currently leads a government-funded research project supported by the Tamil Nadu State Land Use Research Board under the State Planning Commission, aimed at mapping fallow lands across Tamil Nadu using multi-temporal remote sensing analysis. The project contributes to evidence-based land use planning and supports sustainable agricultural development.

data.nf11:
Kirti Sahu is internationally recognized for his pioneering contributions to the microphysics of raindrops, addressing one of the most enduring challenges in environmental science, accurate rainfall prediction. Despite decades of research, reliable forecasting remains elusive, largely due to the poor understanding of raindrop size and shape distributions, which are central to radar-based rainfall estimation. In the absence of this knowledge, current models rely heavily on empirical or ad hoc assumptions, limiting their predictive capability. Sahu has fundamentally transformed this landscape by establishing a first-of-its-kind experimental facility that reproduces the continuously evolving atmospheric conditions raindrops encounter as they descend from clouds to the ground. This unique platform, unparalleled globally, enables, for the first time, systematic measurement of raindrop size and shape distributions as a function of altitude. His results provide critical inputs for next-generation weather prediction and climate models and have shown remarkable agreement with high-quality in-situ observations from the CAIPEEX project, a major Government of India initiative involving extensive and costly field campaigns.

Beyond atmospheric science, Sahu has made equally impactful contributions to industrial fluid dynamics, particularly in understanding mixing processes that underpin a wide range of chemical and energy systems. Sahu's research on bubble dynamics helps identify optimal operating parameters for reactors and improve their design. He discovered two new hydrodynamic instabilities that substantially changed our understanding of stratified flows, benefiting many practical applications; e.g., Bharat Heavy Electricals Limited has been using his findings to predict the metal temperature of industrial boilers. 

In summary, Sahu’s research exemplifies a rare and powerful integration of fundamental science with real-world applications. His work not only resolves long-standing scientific questions but also delivers transformative technologies and methodologies with far-reaching implications for weather forecasting, climate science, and a wide range of engineering applications.

Impact/Outcome of the Work Done by the Nominee :

Kirti Sahu is widely recognized for his groundbreaking research on raindrop dynamics. Accurate rainfall prediction is one of the grand challenges in environmental research due to its relevance in understanding climate change and its accompanying socio-economic impacts. However, the accuracy of rainfall prediction is far from perfect. The distribution of raindrop shapes and sizes in the atmosphere is essential in estimating rainfall using weather radar. This crucial yet unknown aspect currently forces scientists to make ad-hoc assumptions to predict rainfall. One of the critical limitations in rainfall modelling is the lack of fundamental knowledge of the microphysical processes under actual atmospheric conditions. To address the challenges, Kirti Sahu has established a one-of-a-kind experimental facility that mimics the continuously changing atmospheric conditions encountered by raindrops as they descend from clouds to the ground (Patent Number: 484707; Grant Date: 18/12/2023). It is noteworthy that no comparable facility currently exists anywhere in the world. His pioneering research provides much-needed information about the shape of raindrops and their size distributions as a function of altitude. This will be an enabling factor for making more accurate weather forecasting and climate modelling. Sahu’s original research, utilizing his patented technology, exhibited remarkable agreement with rainfall predictions from the classical Marshall-Palmer model for steady rain, as well as with in-situ measurements collected by the CAIPEEX project, an initiative by the Ministry of Earth Sciences, that measures rainfall across various regions in India (Journal of the Atmospheric Sciences, 2025).

His other work addresses mixing, a key feature in most modern industrial processes. For instance, the efficiency of bubble and spray column reactors is governed by the mixing and reaction rates, which are determined by the associated gas-liquid flow. Sahu's work on bubble dynamics helps identify optimal operating parameters for reactors and improve their design. His discovery of two new hydrodynamic instabilities in stratified flows benefited many practical applications, including flow control in refineries and static mixers. Bharat Heavy Electricals Limited (BHEL) boilers design team has taken up Sahu's study on predicting the metal temperature of reheater header tubes of industrial boilers for future implementation. Air Liquide, France, also used his findings to establish counter-current flow in structured packing.

Some of his accomplishments are detailed below.

Raindrop dynamics: The shape and size of raindrops are influenced by coupled microphysical processes, such as coalescence, fragmentation, and condensation/evaporation, which are further affected by temperature, humidity gradients, and air currents. It is to be noted that neglecting droplet coalescence and breakup alone can result in more than 10% error in rainfall modelling. Unfortunately, for numerous reasons, India experiences high rainfall variability, making it a distinct and complex process compared to the rest of the world. Current rainfall models rely on empirical relationships between rainfall rate and droplet diameter, assuming droplets are spherical or ellipsoidal. This assumption introduces significant errors, especially in high-rainfall regions. Moreover, most raindrop size measurements are performed on the ground or near clouds, yet weather radars require this information in the bulk air region. Sahu has established a unique experimental facility that mimics the dynamic atmospheric conditions of raindrops descending from cloud to ground (Patent Number: 484707; Grant Date: 18/12/2023). He uses a new paradigm of machine-learning-based holography technique to determine the three-dimensional shape, velocity, and size distributions of raindrops. Some of his significant research findings are (i) the demonstration of shape oscillations of raindrops and the discovery of a new breakup mode under swirl air current (Journal of Fluid Mechanics, 2023, 2022; International Journal of Multiphase Flow, 2020, Physical Review E, 2019), (ii) modelling of oblique collision of two unequal-sized droplets (Physics of Fluids, 2021), (iii) distinguishing the dynamics of a hollow drop from a regular drop (Theoretical and Computational Fluid Dynamics, 2020), and (iv) fundamental understanding of phase change (Journal of Fluid Mechanics, 2026) and nucleation of water droplets during freezing (Journal of Fluid Mechanics, 2025; Physical Review Fluids, 2023). These findings would resolve several anomalies in cloud and raindrop formation and improve weather radar and artificial rain technologies.

Bubble dynamics: Gas sparging is used to enhance heat and mass transfer in various chemical and petrochemical processes. Despite being a classic subject that has intrigued scientists for centuries, the nominee was the first to produce a regime map demarcating different bubble rise behaviours, including the previously unknown break-up bubbles (Nature Communications, 2015; Physics of Fluids, 2017). Sahu's work helps establish optimal operating conditions to achieve better mixing and higher reaction efficiency, thereby improving the design of bubble column reactors. Bubble motion is also a good indicator of underlying seismic conditions (Royal Society Open Science, 2024). Sahu's research has garnered global attention, with researchers incorporating his findings to enhance seismic predictions in regions prone to volcanic activity (doi: 10.1007/s42241-018-0141-3). For this reason alone, his work is expected to change how we select sites for large-scale civil structures, such as underground tunnels and power plants in earthquake-prone areas. Sahu is a pioneer in the study of bubble dynamics involving “self-rewetting” fluids that exhibit non-monotonic surface tension-temperature relationships. In a series of seminal papers (Langmuir, 2014; Journal of Fluid Mechanics, 2015, 2019, 2021), he has explained several strange dynamics, such as super-spreading behaviour, motion reversal and bubble arrest, which are unique to this class of fluids, and help to improve the design of thermal management devices for space applications.

Stratified flows: The nominee illustrated how spatial and temporal variations in viscosity can profoundly and unexpectedly alter fluid flow. In a series of papers, Sahu conceptualized and demonstrated two new hydrodynamic instabilities that substantially changed our understanding of this subject (Journal of Fluid Mechanics, 2012, 2016, 2021). These works established a new research field and contributed to our comprehension of core-annular pipe flows relevant to the oil and gas industries (Annual Review of Fluid Mechanics, 2014; a highly sought-after publication for fluid dynamics researchers). Sahu's findings provide guidelines for managing large-scale industrial flows.

Direct applications-based studies: Sahu has developed a membrane-less paper-based fuel cell, a green technology that utilises capillary phenomena and microfluidics concepts. The ability to illuminate an LED for 40 minutes demonstrates its potential as an alternative to batteries to power micro-analytical devices (Patent Number: 504402; Grant Date: 29/01/2024).

Sahu has been an outstanding researcher throughout his research career, starting from his PhD days. He answered a long-standing discrepancy between theory and experimental observations of the transition to turbulence in pipe flow, which had persisted since the Reynolds experiment in 1883. He found that although the critical Reynolds number for linear instability of the laminar flow in a straight pipe is infinite, it is finite for a divergent pipe with even the slightest divergence angle (Journal of Fluid Mechanics, 2005). This finding offers a possible instability mechanism for small-scale flows. Flow in microreactors is usually laminar, and chemical reactions are driven by slow diffusive mass transfer, a significant drawback in the practical implementation of such reactors even today. Sahu’s discovery of a novel instability mechanism, triggered caused by a slight change in geometry, has been shown to enhance microreactor performance by an order of magnitude, significantly improving mixing and accelerating reaction rates in low Reynolds number regimes.

In summary, Sahu’s research represents a powerful and enduring integration of fundamental science with real-world applications, with an impact that will stand the test of time. His work not only resolves long-standing fundamental questions but also delivers transformative technologies and methodologies with far-reaching implications for weather forecasting, climate science, and a broad spectrum of engineering applications.

Anupam’s research influenced multiple domains, including EDA, Cyber Security, Embedded Systems and PQC.

During his doctoral studies at RWTH Aachen, Germany, Anupam worked on processor description language LISA, which led to a spin-off, and subsequently was acquired by Synopsys in 2010, which is used for last two decades as one of their flagship products. Later, during his research visit to EPFL, Switzerland in 2014, he contributed to Majority-based logic synthesis, which was licensed by Synopsys and became part of their logic synthesis flow.

As a faculty at NTU, Singapore, Anupam’s research work on In-Memory Computing led to worldwide research attention and was covered by media houses such as Asian Scientist, Straits Times and The Economist.

Anupam’s research on cyber-security led to award-winning research, including side-channel attacks on standardized post-quantum cryptographic primitives, first malware demonstration for Quantum computers and detailed taxonomy of adversarial attacks – which was cited by NIST, USA. Anupam’s works on Quantum-safe Migration led to joint whitepaper with Singapore’s Cyber-Security Agency and Mastercard, USA.

Anupam published 300+ papers in top-ranked IEEE/ACM conferences and journals, acted as the solo lead editor for Springer Handbook on Computer Architectures and organized major conferences (VLSI-SoC, VLSI Design Conference, Indocrypt, SPACE) as Technical Program Chair/General Chair. His research is cited more than 10,000 times as per google scholar.

So far, Anupam has supervised 12 PhD students, 25+ post-doctoral researchers and numerous Master’s/Bachelor’s thesis students. Research members from his team are holding faculty positions in Europe (KU Leuven, Belgium; Groningen University, Netherlands; Queen’s University, Belfast) and Asia (Indian Statistical Institute, Kolkata; IIT Mandi; IIT Tirupati; IIT Jammu). His students are part of large organizations (Amazon, Meta, LinkedIn) and are leading startups in C-suite roles.

His research have been funded by government organizations such as, EU, DFG (Germany), MoE (Singapore), NRF (Singapore) and private companies such as, Synopsys, Xilinx, Google, Amazon, BMW.

Anupam is co-founder and/or technology advisor of 5 startups in the domains of Internet-of-Things (IoT), Post-Quantum Cryptography, Deepfake Detection, AI-based Hardware Design and Automated Driving Assistance Systems. These startups raised multiple rounds of institutional funding, served large customers, received awards such as induction in Singapore’s national incubation programs and got listed in Singapore’s top 20 AI companies to watch.

The prestigious IIT Bombay International Award celebrates ‘researchers and academicians whose work over the past decade has had a significant real-world impact’. This is most apt for Anupam, who, as an academician, rolled out new courses, developed new knowledge, translated those to high-impact commercial ventures and mentored high-calibre researchers across the world.

His impact in academic research is visible through multiple award-winning papers, large number of citations and pioneering works in multiple research domains – such as first malware for Quantum computers, first demonstrated backdoor attack on PQC, first prototype of ternary arithmetic circuits in non-volatile memory devices, axiomatization of majority Boolean algebra and development of a specification language for modelling  embedded proecssors. Beyond publishing papers in highly coveted journals and conferenes, he took painstaking efforts to compile a Handbook of Computer Architectures, containing 40 chapters with collaboration of 100+ researchers across the world, which has since been downloaded more than 60,000 times. His recent advances in Post-Quantum Cryptography not only influences NIST’s standardization process but, led to collaboration with BSSN (Indonesia), CDOT (India), Cyber Security Agency (Singapore) and Mastercard (USA) to roll out well-defined plans for Quantum-safe Migration.

What distinguishes Anupam from other academic researchers, however, is his acumen to identify high-impact real-world problems and zeal to pursue technology commercialization. In the last seven years, he spearheaded creation of 5 spinoffs, which are led by students from his research group. The spinoffs are registered in Singapore and India, with customers in multiple countries, such as - UK, Thailand, Singapore, Indonesia, Malaysia, Australia, Canada – clearly demonstrating a global reach of his technology. The spinoffs serve diverse industry sectors – Deepfaic and PQStation are in the cyber-security; Sapience Automata and Datakrew are in Intelligent Automotive; and ChipForge is set to revolutionize hardware design by using AI. This breadth of technology represents Anupam’s deep understanding of the technology foundation and how it is adopted through various channels.

In short, Anupam has influenced multiple, diverse research domains with his work that not only created new knowledge but, also translated and impacted society at large. This is a rare example of industry-academia bridge that he could successfully and repeatedly demonstrate in his academic journey.

data.nf11: Arvind has made fundamental contributions to real-time scheduling which is concerned with the problem of allocating computational resources to safety-critical applications with hard deadlines. Recently, Arvind explored the problem of resource allocation (wireless bandwidth and server computation) in mobile edge computing systems, where mobile end-devices like autonomous vehicles wirelessly offload compute-intensive workload to edge servers for benefits including energy savings. These problems are NP-Hard, and unlike classical Knapsack, additional complexity arises due to the opportunity to forward offloaded workload to alternate edge servers using backhaul networks. Arvind has developed the first approximation algorithms for variants of this problem using a method called Local Ratio Technique (publications in RTSS2024, RTSS2025 and RTAS2026). The algorithms have constant factor approximations, extremely efficient runtimes (e.g., linear), and the best obtained approximation under deterministic setting of 1/(2+e), e>0, is tight and cannot be improved unless a 1993 result for the much simpler Generalized Assignment Problem is improved. Between 2013-2020, Arvind explored the problem of mixed-criticality real-time scheduling with application in domains like automotive and avionics, where workload criticality is related to safety assurance in certification. In 2015 this work closed an important problem that had remained open since 2007; developed an optimal scheduling algorithm for multi-core processors (RTSS2015). The algorithm has a speed-up bound of 4/3 (factor by which speed of each core is increased to ensure schedulability of any feasible workload), and this bound is tight. Since 2020, Arvind worked on the problem of detecting Out-of-Distribution (OOD) data in machine-learning based CPS. Reasoning about OOD is essential, because the output of the learnt model for such data is often not easy to predict. Focussing on covariate shift OOD such as those arising from meteorological variations in images, Arvind developed novel neurosymbolic methods for encoding the inputs to lower-dimensional embeddings with minimal supervision (ICML2025, AAAI2025, EMSOFT2021).
data.nf14:
From 2021, Arvind is a co-Director for the National Research Foundation, Singapore, research program “DesCartes: Intelligent Modelling for Decision-Making in Critical Urban Systems” (https://descartes.cnrsatcreate.cnrs.fr). It is a S$56 million dollar initiative under the Campus for Research Excellence and Technological Enterprise (CREATE). This program is a large collaboration between more than 17 partner organizations and universities from France and Singapore, led by the French National Centre for Scientific Research (CNRS). This program is well recognized for its research in Hybrid (data+physics based) Artificial Intelligence (AI). It is developing foundational technologies in Hybrid & Safe AI and also applying them across a variety of CPS use-cases including drones, infrastructure monitoring and digital energy systems. See: https://descartes.cnrsatcreate.cnrs.fr/use-cases/  https://descartes.cnrsatcreate.cnrs.fr/news/ 

The domain of CPS mainly comprises applications that are safety-critical in nature. As a consequence, practical impact arising from theoretical and algorithmic studies such as those conducted by Arvind can take significant amounts of time and require collaborative efforts across institutions. One such noteworthy impact is the development of RT-Xen (https://sites.google.com/site/realtimexen), which is a real-time virtualisation framework based on the open-source hypervisor technology Xen (https://www.xenproject.org). Xen originated from the University of Cambridge, United Kingdom, and its board members include ARM, AMD Xilinx, Amazon AWS and several automotive companies. RT-Xen, which was co-developed since 2010 by a team from the University of Pennsylvania and Washington University at St. Louis, United States of America (USA), is incorporated as an experimental scheduler in the mainstream Xen 4.5 codebase since 2016 (https://wiki.xenproject.org/wiki/RTDS-Based-Scheduler?ref=xenproject.org). One of the resource allocation algorithms implemented in RT-Xen is a real-time compositional framework for multi-core processors developed in Arvind’s PhD thesis (relevant publications in ECRTS2008 and Springer Real-Time Systems 2009).
data.nf16:
Arvind has made fundamental theoretical contributions in the area of real-time systems, particularly in resource allocation problems in the context of time-critical and safety-critical applications. His work has developed constant-factor approximation algorithms with tight ratios and linear runtime complexity for mobile edge computing systems, and closed the problem of obtaining a speed-up optimal scheduling algorithm for mixed-criticality systems on multi-core processors. Arvind’s work has also had significant practical impact, and a couple of notable achievements include: 1) Some of his PhD work has found its way into mainstream Xen hypervisors, which is a popular hypervisor technology in the market today. 2) He was also closely involved in the “Autonomous Electric Bus” project in NTU which was in collaboration with Volvo, Sweden. This project received a significant amount of media attention for its R&D novelty in the context of Singapore (https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=SnvUYheellE), and also received the prestigious NTU Nanyang Award for Innovation & Entrepreneurship in 2020. 

Arvind is a well-recognized member of the CPS community for more than two decades, and has several leadership roles to his credit. He was nominated to be the Program Chair for RTSS 2022 by the IEEE Technical Community on Real-Time Systems. This is a very prestigious role at a premier conference, and the nomination is a clear testament to Arvind’s research contributions. Similarly, the Steering Committee of nominated him to the role of Program co-Chair for IEEE/ACM ICCPS 2025 which is another premier conference in the broader CPS area, a clear recognition of his contributions even in the wider community. He has also won Best Paper and Best Student Paper awards at top-tier conferences in CPS. In Singapore, he has been leading several research efforts in the field, including as a co-Director for the program DesCartes (https://descartes.cnrsatcreate.cnrs.fr), which is a collaborative initiative between Singapore and France.

data.nf11:
Erik Cambria has been a trailblazer in Singapore’s AI landscape since 2011, when he began a research journey that laid the foundation for bridging human cognition and machine intelligence. Central to his contributions is a neurosymbolic AI framework, which combines the strengths of symbolic AI and deep learning. His work emphasizes accuracy, usability, interpretability, explainability, and trustworthiness—key factors in building ethical and reliable AI systems.

This framework has been applied to a range of transformative domains. In mental health, he has worked with the Ministry of Health on stress, depression, suicidal ideation, and mental disorder detection. His research also contributes to climate change mitigation, particularly in the analysis and prediction of weather disasters and wildfires. In finance, his AI models support socially responsible investing, with a focus on green finance and corporate sustainability. Beyond research, Professor Cambria is a passionate advocate for AI education. At NTU, he has played a leading role in training future AI practitioners and researchers. He regularly speaks at major international conferences, organizes special issues on explainable AI, and advises industry leaders through executive training programs like the CXO GenAI Masterclass and the NVIDIA AI Technology Symposium. A recent highlight of his educational work is the creation of NTU’s AI Literacy course (PGR ICC), designed for PhD students from all disciplines. This course demystifies AI, fosters interdisciplinary learning, and equips students to apply AI in their respective fields. Professor Cambria is also committed to advancing AI adoption among SMEs in Singapore. He developed a tailored AI adoption roadmap and supports local businesses through his startups, SenticNet (business.sentic.net) and finaXai (finaxai.com), and collaborated with AI Singapore on building SEA-LION (sentic.net/sea-lion.pdf). His efforts have empowered many SMEs to embrace AI, driving innovation and supporting Singapore’s digital transformation goals.
data.nf12: Erik Cambria has received numerous prestigious awards recognizing his contributions to AI research and education. He was honored with the 2024 COE Research Award – Special Mention and the 2024 NTU Long Service Award. He has been consistently featured in elite rankings, including Clarivate’s Highly Cited Researchers List (top 1% globally since 2022), the AI 2000 Most Influential Scholar Honorable Mention in AAAI/IJCAI since 2022, and Stanford University’s List of the World’s Top 2% Scientists since 2021. His early career achievements include the 2020 Nanyang Research Award (Young Investigator), the 2019 IEEE CIS Outstanding Early Career Award, and being named one of AI’s 10 to Watch by IEEE Intelligent Systems in 2018. That same year, Forbes cited him among the top 5 AI researchers shaping the future. Cambria's research excellence has also been recognized with several paper awards, including the 2025 IEEE CIS TAI Outstanding Paper Award, the 2025 IEEE SSCI Best Paper Award, and the Best Poster Award at the ICML 2024 AI4Science Workshop.

data.nf14: Erik Cambria has been a trailblazer in Singapore's AI landscape since 2011, when he embarked on his research journey, recently encapsulated in his latest book on natural language understanding. This foundational work set the stage for his ongoing efforts to bridge the gap between human cognition and machine intelligence. Over the years, he has dedicated himself to advancing his neurosymbolic AI framework, a cutting-edge approach that combines the strengths of symbolic AI and deep learning. His work has centered on refining the accuracy, usability, interpretability, explainability, and trustworthiness of AI systems, addressing critical challenges that ensure AI technologies are not only powerful but also reliable and ethical. He has also founded two companies, SenticNet (business.sentic.net) and finaXai (finaxai.com), and collaborated with AI Singapore on building SEA-LION (sentic.net/sea-lion.pdf). His framework has been applied to various transformative applications, including: 1) Mental Health: collaborating with the Ministry of Health (MOH) on stress and depression detection, but also suicidal ideation and mental disorder detection; 2) Climate Change Mitigation: with particular focus on weather disasters and wildfires; 3) Socially Responsible Investing: targeting green finance and corporate sustainability.
data.nf16: In recent years, AI has shown immense promise in advancing science and society. However, despite its strength in classification and pattern recognition, AI continues to face challenges in tasks requiring commonsense reasoning—particularly in natural language understanding. Recognizing these limitations, Professor Erik Cambria has led pioneering work to transition traditional AI into explainable AI (XAI), addressing key issues such as trustworthiness, interpretability, explainability, and reproducibility. To tackle these challenges, he introduced a novel commonsense-based neurosymbolic framework that combines unsupervised subsymbolic techniques—like autoregressive language models and kernel methods—with symbolic representations. This approach enables natural language to be transformed into a structured protolanguage, allowing for interpretable and fully explainable extraction of meaning from text. Unlike existing post-hoc interpretability methods that merely highlight correlations between inputs and outputs, Cambria emphasizes faithfulness (accurately reflecting model reasoning) and plausibility (resonating with human understanding). His vision for AI goes beyond explainability alone. He focuses on creating human-in-the-loop and human-in-command systems, enabling people to guide and collaborate with AI through active learning and decision intelligence frameworks. His roadmap for responsible AI development centers on fairness, accountability, transparency, and privacy—principles that should be embedded from the design phase. To broaden access and impact, he has made these tools freely available to researchers, developers, and even non-AI experts. Resources include downloadable software, open-source code, and no-code AI services through SenticNet, all hosted on [sentic.net](https://sentic.net). Additionally, his YouTube channel provides regular updates and educational content to promote public understanding of XAI. Professor Cambria’s research not only contributes groundbreaking methods to AI but also advances its ethical integration across sectors such as medicine, education, and policy.

The scientific contributions of Prof. Sachin Kumar center on advancing the understanding, prevention, and control of viral diseases, particularly those affecting poultry and zoonotic transmission. His research integrates molecular virology, immunology, and translational approaches to address critical challenges in viral pathogenesis, diagnostics, and vaccine development.
A major contribution lies in the development and application of reverse genetics systems for avian viruses, especially Newcastle disease virus (NDV) and avian paramyxoviruses. His work has enabled the engineering of viral vectors for vaccine delivery, including recombinant viruses expressing heterologous antigens, significantly advancing next-generation vaccine platforms. He has also contributed to DNA vaccine design and viral vector-based immunization strategies, improving protective immunity in animal models.
Prof. Kumar’s research has provided key insights into virus–host interactions, particularly the role of host metabolic pathways, cytokine responses, and cellular stress mechanisms in viral replication. His studies on Japanese encephalitis virus (JEV) and NDV have elucidated how viruses exploit glycolysis, autophagy, and oxidative stress pathways, identifying potential host-directed therapeutic targets. His work on antiviral compounds, including drug repurposing and natural molecules, further supports translational applications.
Another significant area is the development of rapid and sensitive diagnostic tools, including molecular assays and biosensors for viral detection. These innovations are crucial for early disease surveillance and outbreak management, especially in resource-limited settings.
His contributions also extend to epidemiology and viral evolution, including characterization of emerging and re-emerging viral strains in India and globally. He has investigated cross-species transmission, viral diversity, and genomic adaptations, enhancing preparedness for zoonotic threats.
With nearly 200 publications and substantial impact in virology and veterinary sciences, his work bridges fundamental research and applied solutions, contributing to animal health, public health, and food security.

Professor Sachin Kumar is a distinguished scientist in the field of molecular virology and biotechnology whose research has significantly advanced the understanding of emerging and transboundary viral diseases affecting animals and humans. His work focuses on viral pathogenesis, vaccine development, molecular diagnostics, and host–virus interactions, with particular emphasis on economically important pathogens such as Newcastle disease virus, Japanese encephalitis virus, and African swine fever virus.
            Professor Kumar has made substantial contributions to the development of recombinant viral vectors, innovative diagnostic approaches, and antiviral strategies. His research has elucidated key molecular mechanisms governing viral replication, metabolic regulation during infection, and host immune responses. In addition, he has contributed to the development of biosensor-based and molecular diagnostic platforms for rapid detection of viral pathogens and has actively explored reverse genetics approaches for vaccine development.
            With an exceptional publication record of over 190 peer-reviewed papers, including nearly 90 as corresponding author, his work has appeared in leading international journals and has accumulated a high cumulative impact factor. His contributions also include several book chapters and scholarly works on viral diagnostics, vaccine design, and bioreactor technologies for viral vaccine production.
            Beyond research, Professor Kumar has mentored numerous doctoral and master’s students and actively contributes to the scientific community as a reviewer and editor for numerous international journals. His sustained contributions have had a lasting impact on virology research and the development of strategies to combat emerging infectious diseases.

data.nf11:
Prof Joel Yang of SUTD has made significant and sustained contributions in nanoscience and technology at the intersections of photonics, materials science, and advanced manufacturing. His key contributions are in the areas of structural coloration and nanoscale 3D lithography, through the introduction of new concepts with wide applicability.

In particular, Prof Yang demonstrated fundamentally new mechanisms in photonic structural colors where colors are achieved without dyes of pigments. Traditionally, structural colors are based on large areas and repeating structures, such as in thin film interference and diffractive effects.  Instead, he demonstrated that individual nanostructures can operate as color elements, thus enabling pixels that are potentially sub-wavelength in dimension. These structures act as building blocks for color generation and spectral control of light in highly miniaturized form factors. Based on physical mechanism such as surface plasmon or Mie resonances, it is the geometric designs that determine the wavelength-selective behavior of these individual nanostructures.

The key implication is the redefinition of the concept of color generation: Controlling the size and shapes of the nanostructure pixels determine its color to achieve structural color filters/prints/spectrometers at resolutions exceeding the optical diffraction limit.  He has since demonstrated such structural color prints in a wide range of material classes (relying on different physical mechanisms), i.e. metals (plasmonics), high-index dielectrics (Mie resonances), low index dielectrics (truncated waveguides). These approaches broaden the design space for multiplexing sets of prints using polarization, incident angle, and holographic channels for selection, suited for encoding and encrypting optical information.

Prof Yang is has further made pioneering advances in nanoscale 3D lithography through Two-Photon Polymerization Lithography (TPL). Here, he introduced the "print and shrink" strategy that overcomes the resolution and material limitations in TPL to achieve high-resolution 3D prints that control and manipulate the propagation of light in optical and photonic devices.  Notable novel designs of microscale optical elements achieved using TPL include blazed diffractive optical elements, achromatic multilayer metalenses and spiral phase plates combined with structural colors in a single lithographic step, enabling holographic color prints, color vortex beams, and high-resolution light-field prints. Through materials innovation in the form of custom resins for TPL, new inorganic materials can now be printed, e.g. high-index Titania and Glass, which fills the capability gap in achieving high resolution nanostructures with inorganic materials.  

Prof Yang’s work in these areas have made impact internationally in the fields of optics and photonics, semiconducor technology, and quantum devices. His impact is seen through granted patents, publications in high-impact journals (e.g. Science, Science Advances, Nature Photonics, Nature Nanotechnology), competitive research funding, and keynote talks at major international conferences. He has also played a key role in building interdisciplinary research capabilities in his roles as Cleanroom Director and Associate Head of Pillar for Research & Advancement at SUTD, mentoring students and early-career researchers, and fostering collaborations with industry and international partners. His work aligns strongly with the objectives of the IITB International Award, making him a highly deserving nominee.

Seminal work and accompanying patent in 2012 demonstrating high-resolution color printing at 100,000 dpi (2-3 orders of magnitude higher than state-of-the-art color printers) using silver nanodisks on a backreflector. This work was followed with more economical alternatives of printing using Aluminum, and demonstration of polarization dependence of colors in 2014:
• “Printing Color at the Optical Diffraction Limit”, Nature Nanotechnology (2012) 7, 557–561 doi:10.1038/nnano.2012.128 (Featured on Cover of journal)

Extension of work to enhance the color saturation (vividness) using high-index dielectrics, i.e. Silicon, and Mie resonances by fabricating Si nanodisks on a substrate. He discovered surprisingly the fulfilment of 1st and 2nd Kerker’s conditions that usually requires particles to be suspended in vacuum or a homogeneous medium. These results then led to the patterning of photodetectors in Si that are color-sensitive without the need for color filters: 
• “Printing Beyond sRGB Color Gamut by Mimicking Silicon Nanostructures in Free-Space”, Nano Letters 17 (12), 7620-7628  (2017)

Prof Yang recently extended the high-resolution structural color prints to low-index dielectrics in the form of 3D printed nanopillars in crosslinked resin that act as truncated waveguides, achieved using two-photon polymerization lithography (TPL). Dr. Rahul V. Ralegaonkar has made outstanding and sustained contributions to the field of science and technology, particularly in Sustainable Construction Engineering. With a strong academic foundation comprising B.E., M.E., and a Ph.D. in Energy Efficient Buildings, he brings over two decades of rich experience spanning academics, research, consultancy, and industry. His work reflects a rare integration of scientific innovation with practical implementation, significantly advancing energy-efficient and environmentally responsible construction practices.
Dr. Ralegaonkar’s global exposure, including the prestigious Indo-US BHAVAN Fellowship at the University of Texas and his tenure as Visiting Professor at Liverpool John Moores University, has strengthened international research collaborations. His contributions have been recognized through multiple international and national awards, particularly for developing innovative, energy-efficient construction materials.
A key highlight of his work is the successful development and transfer of sustainable technologies, including patented green construction materials and carbon-minus building design tools. His Green Concrete technology has been effectively implemented by government bodies, demonstrating real-world impact. He has completed several high-value R&D projects funded by national and international agencies, including ongoing projects supported by the Ministry of Coal.
With over 190 research publications, four authored books, and several book chapters with reputed publishers, Dr. Ralegaonkar has significantly enriched scientific literature. As an academic leader at Visvesvaraya National Institute of Technology, Nagpur, he has mentored numerous doctoral, postgraduate, and undergraduate students, fostering the next generation of researchers.
His leadership roles, policy contributions, and extensive outreach through lectures, training programs, and national missions underline his commitment to societal development. Dr. Ralegaonkar’s work exemplifies excellence in science and technology with a strong focus on sustainability, innovation, and impactful knowledge dissemination.

data.nf11: Dr. Shukla’s research is focused on the largest class of cell surface receptors in our body, known as G protein-coupled receptors (GPCRs), which are intricately involved in almost every aspect of human physiology including vision, olfaction, cardiovascular regulation and immune response. Approximately half of the currently prescribed medicines work through GPCRs including those prescribed for heart failure, hypertension and neurological disorders. The overarching theme of Dr. Shukla’s research is to understand the structure, function and regulation of GPCRs, and to leverage this information for designing novel therapeutics with minimized side-effects. Dr. Shukla’s outstanding research work on GPCRs carried out in India has made fundamental contributions in the domain of GPCR biology and has established several important paradigms. Overall, Dr. Shukla’s research has contributed immensely in the ever-exciting domain of GPCR biology and drug discovery, and it has also generated tools and technologies that have directly influenced other related research domains. Moreover, his efforts to establish India in the field of membrane biology on the global arena, inspiring the younger generation to catalyze brain again and reverse brain drain, and his critical contribution in establishing cutting-edge infrastructure for the next generation are indeed commendable and inspiring. Dr. Shukla is also spearheading the SERB-sponsored National cryo-EM facility at IIT Kanpur that is serving the entire structural biology community in the country.
data.nf12: Dr. Shukla has clearly emerged as a scientific leader in the discipline of Biological Sciences in India, and his work is recognized by multiple laurels including the Infosys Prize, Shanti Swarup Bhatnagar Award from CSIR, Fellowship of the Indian National Science Academy, Indian Academy of Sciences and National Academy of Sciences, Khosla National Award from IIT Roorkee, Swarnajayanti Fellowship from DST, the National Bioscience Award from DBT, the Sun Pharma Research Award, CDRI Drug Discovery Award, Rajib Goyal Prize, and the Senior and Intermediate Fellowship of the DBT Wellcome Trust India Alliance. Dr. Shukla’s research has also received several international recognitions including the EMBO Young Investigator Award, the Chemical Biologist award of the International Chemical Biology Society (USA), and the invitation to join the Editorial Advisory Boards of multiple international journals including Molecular Cell, Journal of Biological Chemistry, Trends in Biochemical Sciences, Cell Reports, Molecular Pharmacology, and The British Journal of Pharmacology.
data.nf13: Dr. Shukla’s outstanding research work on GPCRs carried out in India has made fundamental contributions in the domain of GPCR biology and has established several important paradigms. For example, most of the GPCR targeting drugs exhibit severe side-effects limiting their clinical utility, and Dr. Shukla’s work has provided key insights into how selectively engaging a specific pathway through distinct conformations of GPCR signaling complexes may help minimize the side-effects of commonly prescribed medicines (Nature Communications, 2016; Nature Structure & Molecular Biology, 2018; Nature Communications, 2021; Nature Communications, 2024; Nature Communications, 2025). These studies have been very well received in the field as reflected by their extensive citation, and coverage by the experts, for example, in the form of accompanying commentaries and News and Views articles. His laboratory has also discovered a new family of seven transmembrane receptors (7TMRs) that couple and signal exclusively through β-arrestins but not G-proteins (Molecular Cell, 2021; Nature Communications, 2023; Cell, 2024; Molecular Cell, 2025). This finding represents a landmark discovery and changes the concept that all 7TMRs must couple to G-proteins. The impact of this study is underscored by its coverage in Science Signaling journal, and the accompanying interview of Dr. Shukla by Molecular Cell journal. His laboratory has also established a synthetic antibody technology platform to successfully generate designer proteins to probe and modulate GPCR activation, trafficking, and signaling in cellular context (Nature Nontechnology, 2017; JBC, 2020; Nature Communications, 2022). These are excellent research tools that the global GPCR community has been craving for, and Dr. Shukla’s laboratory has already shared these reagents with multiple laboratories across the world. They open-up new frontiers with far-reaching implications for illuminating novel aspects of GPCR biology, and the paper in JBC (2020) describing some of these tools was one of the most downloaded articles, and it was selected as Paper of the Year under Signal-Transduction category by the journal. Dr. Shukla’s work has also demonstrated that even single phosphorylation sites in GPCRs can regulate the interaction and function of β-arrestins, which refines the classical notion that cumulative GPCR phosphorylation is always required for the binding and activation of β-arrestins (Science Advances, 2020; EMBO Reports, 2020). A major conundrum in the field has been the ability of two β-arrestin isoforms to regulate hundreds of GPCRs, and a potential universal mechanism has not been apparent until recently. In multiple collaborative efforts, Dr. Shukla’s laboratory has played a central role in uncovering previously unanticipated and broadly conserved mechanisms driving GPCR-β-arrestin interaction and activation (Cell Reports, 2019; Nature, 2020; Molecular Cell, 2023a; Molecular Cell, 2023b; Cell, 2023a; Cell, 2023b; Science, 2024).
data.nf14: After his return to India, Dr. Shukla decided to work on incredibly challenging but highly rewarding research problems related to membrane protein biology, where India lacked global presence. His research program not only established the country on the global map in this domain but his constant advocacy at multiple levels has also been instrumental in garnering funding and infrastructure in this area including the National cryo-Electron Microscopy facilities approved recently by the Science and Engineering Research Board. His outstanding research performance from Indian soil has inspired many younger scientists working abroad to return back to India, and some of his leadership efforts, inspiration to the next generation, and contribution towards improving the research infrastructure are covered by several News articles (Nature – Turning Point, 2015; JCS – Cell Scientist to watch, 2017; Nature, 2017; Asian Scientists – Asia’s Rising Scientists; Nature India, 2019; Nature, 2019; Nature - World View 2021; Science Signaling, 2023; Nature Cell Biology, 2024).
data.nf16: Dr. Shukla is one of the brightest and most prolific scientists in the country, and his exceptional research on structure and function of G protein-coupled receptors (GPCRs), carried out at IIT Kanpur over the past decade, has made a remarkable contribution at the National and International level. A unique aspect of Dr. Shukla’s academic track-record is his exceptional training pedigree as he carried out his doctoral and post-doctoral research work in the laboratories of three different Nobel laureates in Germany and United States. Despite a lucrative academic career ahead in the United States, Dr. Shukla returned to his motherland a decade ago, and he has spearheaded an exemplary research program that has established our country on the global arena in the broad area of membrane protein structural biology. Dr. Shukla’s research has elucidated the details of how clinically prescribed drugs for a range of human disorders interact with, and regulate, the function of their cognate receptors in the human body. His research has also discovered previously unappreciated mechanisms that GPCRs utilize to receive the information on the outside of the cells and relay the message across the cell membrane. This relay of information across the cell boundary is critical for specific cellular and physiological outcomes such as brain function, cardiovascular regulation and our ability to fight pathogenic organisms. Dr. Shukla’s research program also included designing synthetic proteins such as antibodies that can be utilized to better understand and modulate GPCR functions in human physiology. The research findings from his laboratory at IIT Kanpur have been published in several high-visibility journals including Science, Cell, Nature, Nature Nanotechnology, Molecular Cell, Nature Structure and Molecular Biology, Nature Communications, Science Advances, Cell Reports and EMBO Reports. This international research award will be a most well-deserved and timely recognition for Dr. Shukla’s outstanding research contributions.

data.nf10: Pharmacology, drug safety, toxicity prediction, translational biomedical research, drug discovery
data.nf11: Professor Vetriselvan Subramaniyan has made sustained contributions to pharmacology, drug discovery, translational biomedical research, and drug safety over 15+ years. His work has significantly advanced understanding of drug-induced toxicity mechanisms and improved early prediction of adverse drug reactions, especially hepatotoxicity. By integrating pharmacological science with molecular and cellular approaches, he has strengthened drug safety profiling and supported development of safer therapeutic agents. A key highlight of his recent contribution is collaborative research with the Indian Institute of Technology Bombay (IIT Bombay), demonstrating that antibiotic-induced liver toxicity can be predicted through analysis of drug interactions with liver cell membranes. This paradigm-shifting discovery has been widely reported in The Indian Express, The New Indian Express, IndiaMedToday, Devdiscourse, ThePrint, and IIT Bombay’s official research portal. The study showed that membrane-level drug localization and interaction patterns act as early biomarkers of toxicity risk, offering a major improvement over conventional dose-based toxicity assessment methods. This framework enables earlier and more accurate hepatotoxicity prediction, enhancing preclinical drug safety evaluation. His broader research spans translational biomedical science, converting laboratory findings into clinically relevant applications, particularly in drug screening and toxicity prediction models. He has also contributed significantly to drug discovery by elucidating mechanisms underlying drug efficacy and adverse effects, supporting rational drug design. His interdisciplinary approach integrates pharmacology, molecular biology, and computational sciences to improve predictive accuracy in preclinical evaluation systems. Through academic leadership, postgraduate supervision, and international collaborations, he has played a major role in global capacity building in biomedical sciences. His work continues to influence modern research directions in pharmacology, drug safety assessment, and translational medicine, reinforcing his impact on advancing safer and more effective therapeutic development worldwide.

Technology Transfers (IIT Bombay & Sunway University Collaborative Research)

Membrane-Based Early Toxicity Screening Platform

Contributed to development of a novel membrane-interaction–based toxicity prediction model in collaboration with Indian Institute of Technology Bombay.
This approach enables early detection of drug-induced liver injury (DILI) during preclinical stages, allowing pharmaceutical industries to screen safer drug candidates and reduce late-stage failures.
The method is scalable and can be integrated into drug development pipelines and regulatory safety testing frameworks.

Impact on Safer Drug Design and Pharmaceutical R&D (IIT Bombay & Sunway University Collaborative Research)

The research provides a transformative shift from dose-based to membrane-localization–based toxicity prediction, guiding rational design of less hepatotoxic drugs.
It has strong translational potential for industry adoption in antibiotic development and broader therapeutic areas, helping reduce drug withdrawals and improve patient safety globally

data.nf16:
Global Research Excellence and Impact

Professor Vetriselvan Subramaniyan is a globally recognized scientist with over 400 publications, high citation indices, and inclusion among the top 2% of researchers worldwide. His work consistently addresses critical global health challenges and demonstrates sustained academic excellence and influence.
Transformative Innovation in Drug Safety

His collaborative research with Indian Institute of Technology Bombay introduces a paradigm shift in toxicity prediction by focusing on membrane-level drug interactions. This breakthrough, widely reported in The Indian Express, ThePrint, and The Hindu, enables early detection of hepatotoxicity and improves drug safety evaluation in preclinical stages.
Strong Translational and Industrial Relevance

His work bridges laboratory science and real-world application, contributing to scalable toxicity screening platforms and safer drug design strategies. These innovations have strong potential for adoption in pharmaceutical R&D, reducing late-stage failures and enhancing patient safety globally.
Leadership, Mentorship, and Capacity Building

As a senior academic at Sunway University and former leader at MAHSA University, he has mentored numerous postgraduate and PhD researchers, evaluated doctoral theses internationally, and fostered global collaborations, strengthening biomedical research capacity.
Recognition, Professional Service, and Global Engagement

He is a Fellow of multiple prestigious scientific bodies and an active member of international societies. His roles as keynote speaker, grant recipient, and research leader reflect his commitment to advancing pharmacology and translational medicine on a global platform.
Overall Merit

Professor Vetriselvan’s combination of scientific innovation, translational impact, leadership, and global recognition makes him an outstanding candidate deserving of this prestigious international award.

data.nf10: Wireless Communication, GNSS Security, NavIC Anti-Jamming & Anti- Spoofing , and AI-driven Surveillance
data.nf11:
In my capacity as Dean, I have closely monitored Dr. Shweta Shah’s trajectory, and her contributions to satellite navigation security are nothing short of transformative for our national infrastructure. Her primary impact lies in securing the NavIC (IRNSS) constellation—a task of immense strategic importance. Dr. Shah has moved beyond conventional signal processing to design and implement 4-Element Controlled Reception Pattern Antenna (CRPA) based anti-jamming systems. These systems are essential for ensuring that NavIC remains functional even in the face of sophisticated electronic interference.

Her leadership as a Principal Investigator is equally impressive. She has successfully managed a portfolio of high-stakes research projects funded by ISRO, DST, TEQIP, and the Gujarat Police, with a combined value of 1.68 Cr. A standout achievement from these initiatives is her development of advanced spoofing detection algorithms and a customized health monitoring device for law enforcement personnel. What sets Dr. Shah apart is her ability to bridge the gap between high-level mathematical theory and the rugged, practical requirements of indigenous navigation security. She is not just producing papers; she is building the technological shields that protect our nation’s strategic interests.
data.nf12:
• Strategic Research Leadership: Serves as the Principal Investigator for high-priority projects in GNSS security and NavIC signal processing, funded by premier national research bodies.

• National Standardization: Represents our institute on the NavIC receiver BIS standardization committee, where she helps set the technical benchmarks for the country.

• Institutional Governance: Served a distinguished tenure as the Associate Dean of Student Welfare (SW) at SVNIT Surat (2022–2025), where she significantly improved student engagement and administrative transparency.

• Excellence in Mentorship: Successfully guided seven PhD candidates to completion in rigorous fields such as Microwave Sensing, NavIC Research, and Spectral Efficiency.

•Technical Expert: A sought-after voice in the technical community, frequently serving as an expert speaker for national committees and international startup ecosystems.
data.nf13:
Dr. Shah’s scholarly output is both vast and high-impact, totaling 46 SCI journal papers and 25 international conference papers. Her work, primarily featured in IEEE and other leading journals, has shaped our understanding of MIMO technology, signal processing, and adaptive antenna selection. Beyond the citations, her research has had a tangible impact on the agri-food sector through her innovations in microwave sensing.

Her intellectual property record is equally robust, featuring one device patent and three design patents. However, her most significant impact is seen in her commitment to technology transfer. Through her startup, TruVibe Technology Pvt Ltd, she has commercialized research-grade concepts into field-ready hardware. Products like TruTrack and the "Portable Device and Method For Location Mapping" are prime examples of her ability to translate complex academic theory into commercial-grade, AI-driven navigation solutions. Her technical work on NavIC receiver security is now widely cited, providing a vital layer of protection for both strategic defense and civilian navigation users.
data.nf14:
Beyond the lab, Dr. Shah has been a visionary architect of our academic environment. She was the driving force behind the restructuring of our Space Research and Wireless Technologies curricula. By integrating cutting-edge modules on Artificial Intelligence and Edge Computing, she has single-handedly elevated the global competitiveness of our M.Tech and B.Tech graduates.

Her ability to balance this heavy academic lift with her responsibilities as Associate Dean of Student Welfare (2022–2025) was remarkable. She managed to uphold the highest standards of institutional building while simultaneously launching a deep-tech startup. This rare blend of academic rigor and entrepreneurial drive makes her a unique figure in Indian engineering. She is a true institutional builder who has redefined what it means to be a modern engineering professor.
data.nf16:
Dr. Shweta Shah is the embodiment of the modern engineering leader. Her selection for this award is justified by her profound contribution to India’s technological sovereignty. In an era where signal integrity is a matter of national security, her pioneering work on NavIC anti-jamming and anti-spoofing provides a critical defense against electronic warfare.

What makes Dr. Shah an exceptional candidate is that she does not stop at the "discovery" phase. Through her startup, she has taken the bold step of commercializing her research, ensuring that "Make in India" is a reality in the high-stakes domain of satellite navigation. Her portfolio as a Principal Investigator on strategic projects, combined with her mentorship of seven PhD scholars, demonstrates a holistic impact that is rare in academia.

She has successfully managed multi-crore budgets, shaped national standards, and inspired a new generation of women in STEM. Dr. Shah is not just a researcher; she is a trailblazer whose work secures our infrastructure and elevates our academic standing on the international stage. I recommend her for this prestigious honor without reservation.
